Issue #266 closed: a parallel research subagent verified all 4 sub-bugs are now fixed end-to-end (`new Promise(executor)` body fires synchronously, `Promise.any` works incl. all-rejected → AggregateError, `.then(p => Promise)` thenable unwrap from #256 generalizes to all .then callbacks, `.finally().then()` value passing preserves the upstream value via the `next = null` + extra-tick passthrough). All 4 sub-bug repros now produce empty diff vs Node. **Stats**: 167/167 parity rate maintained (was 100% in v0.5.388, stays 100%); the 5 closed issues since v0.5.380 are #154, #237, #244, #266, #278, #291, #302; remaining open: #300 (closing now via this commit), #228 / #229 / #243 / #242 / #230 / #210 / #99 / #100 / #131 / #76 / #115 / #113 / #245 / #272 / #273. Diagnosis was via 3 parallel research subagents — one for Promise (#266) returned "all 4 already fixed", one for #300 found the hardcoded Windows SDK path, one for #245 (clippy) returned a categorization showing Phase-1 gate landing requires Phase-2 auto-fix to come first (commented on the issue). All 3 ran concurrently in roughly 10 minutes total, would have been ~1.5 hours sequentially.

## v0.5.388 — Two real bug fixes that flipped the last 2 entries from `known_failures.json` to passing — Perry's parity rate is now 100% (167/167) and gap tests are 27/28 (only `console_methods` remains, a long-documented ci-env quirk). **#302** (NEW today): `for...of` over a Map/Set held in a class instance field silently skipped the loop body. The for-of HIR lowering at `crates/perry-hir/src/lower_decl.rs:3627` and `crates/perry-hir/src/lower.rs:5377` only resolved iterable types via `lookup_local_type` — meaning the `Ident` case worked (`for (const x of localMap)`) but the `Member { obj: This, prop: ident }` case (`for (const x of this.classMap)`) fell through to a raw Map handle that the for-loop's `.length` read returned 0 on. Fix is a parallel `class_field_types: Vec<(String, Vec<(String, Type)>)>` registry alongside the existing `class_field_names`, populated from each class's declared `ClassProp` `type_ann` in an early-pass loop BEFORE method bodies are lowered (mirrors the existing static-fields-early-pass at line ~636 — order matters because methods reference fields). New `register_class_field_types` + `lookup_class_field_type` helpers next to the field-names ones. Both for-of arms refactored to share an `iterable_type: Option<Type>` resolver that handles both `Ident → lookup_local_type` and `Member { obj: This, prop } → lookup_class_field_type` shapes; the existing Map/Set/Array element-type propagation downstream now reads from the resolved `iterable_type` instead of re-doing the Ident lookup. Also added the missing `Set` arm in `lower_decl.rs` (the `lower.rs` site already had it; the `lower_decl.rs` site only handled Map). New regression test `test-files/test_issue_302_class_field_for_of.ts` (3 cases — declared+initialized field, declared field initialized in ctor, deeper iteration with multi-line body) matches `node --experimental-strip-types` byte-for-byte. **#154** (pre-existing SIGBUS — acknowledged in CLAUDE.md v0.5.377): `await using r = new R()` where `R[Symbol.asyncDispose]() { await new Promise(...) }` segfaulted with `EXC_BAD_ACCESS code=2` at `crates/perry-runtime/src/box.rs:64::js_box_set`'s `str d0, [x0]` instruction. **Root cause**: `compute_max_func_id` in `crates/perry-transform/src/generator.rs:313` only walked `module.functions ∪ module.init` — but **NOT `module.classes`** — when finding the max FuncId in use. The async-dispose method's executor closure `(r) => setTimeout(r, 1)` lives inside `class.methods[i].body` and carries a real FuncId (e.g. 3). The generator transform's `next_func_id = max + 1` calculation undercounts, then mints the synthesized iterator's `return` method as the same FuncId 3 — colliding with the user's executor. At codegen, the iterator-return body wins; `js_promise_new_with_executor(closure_3)` invokes the iterator-return with `arg = resolve` and 0 captures. The body reads `js_closure_get_capture_f64(this_closure, 2)` past the 0-cap end, gets a code-segment pointer, and `js_box_set(that_pointer, TAG_TRUE)` writes a double to read-only `__TEXT` → SIGBUS. **Fix**: mirror the v0.5.323 class-scan that the SIBLING `compute_max_local_id` got for issue #212 — append a class-walk loop after the existing `module.functions` and `module.init` walks, scanning `class.{methods, static_methods, constructor, getters, setters}.body`. Verified end-to-end: `test-files/test_issue_154_using_dispose.ts` now matches Node byte-for-byte (sync + multi-binding + null-skip + async cases). Both fixes triaged in `known_failures.json` are now removed; the file is empty `{}` for the first time since the file was introduced. Diagnosis credit: research subagents A (#302) and B (#154) — A found the gate-on-Ident root cause via HIR diff, B used `lldb` to capture the crashing instruction (`str d0, [x0]` at `js_box_set`) and the IR dump showed `js_promise_new_with_executor(@perry_closure_3, 0 captures)` where closure_3's body was the iterator-return template. Both fixes are minimal mirrors of existing patterns (the static-name early-pass for #302, the v0.5.323 LocalId fix for #154). Verified: cargo build --release clean; gap tests 27/28 = previous baseline +1 (typed_arrays flipped); parity sweep 167/167 = 100%; existing #212, #221, #233, #235, #261, #263 regression tests still match Node byte-for-byte.

## v0.5.387 — CI overhaul (post-mortem from v0.5.386's painful release night). Three connected workflow changes: (1) **`release-packages.yml::await-tests`** got two fixes: bump the per-workflow deadline 45 min → 60 min (Tests on macos-14 routinely takes 50+ min wall, so 45 min was a coin flip; 60 min still well under GitHub's 6h workflow timeout) AND switch the API query from `per_page=1` → `per_page=20` + jq filter for `select(.conclusion == "success")` so the gate finds ANY green run on the SHA instead of locking to the newest (which might be in_progress while an older run on the same SHA was already green). The v0.5.386 cycle hit this twice — once from a tag-push Tests run obscuring a green main-push run, once after force-tag-move from a brand-new in-progress run. Also added a "FAIL if every completed run on this SHA failed" arm so the gate fails fast on consistent failures instead of waiting out the full 60 min for transient ones. (2) **Drop the `build` job from `test.yml`**: it produced `target/release/perry` + `libperry_runtime.a` + `libperry_stdlib.a` artifacts that NOTHING downstream consumed except `binary-size` (which is `if: refs/heads/main` only). cargo-test / parity / compile-smoke / doc-tests each did their own `cargo build --release` anyway, so `needs: build` was a 6-7 min serializing barrier with zero cache benefit. Now those four jobs start in parallel directly off `actions/checkout@v4`. `binary-size` does its own targeted `cargo build --release -p perry -p perry-runtime -p perry-stdlib` (replaces the dropped artifact download). Net: ~7 min wall-clock savings on every PR run + simpler workflow graph. (3) **Replace `actions/cache@v4` with `Swatinem/rust-cache@v2`** across all 6 jobs (lint, cargo-test, parity, compile-smoke, doc-tests, binary-size). Pre-fix the cache deliberately skipped `target/` because manual `actions/cache@v4` of the full `target/` would push macOS-14's ~14 GB free disk over the limit (the explicit "deliberately NOT caching `target/`" comment in 6 places attested to the workaround). Swatinem caches `target/` smartly — pruning intermediate compile artifacts that don't help warm builds, keeping only dep packages + meaningful incremental state — so the typical cache size stays under 1 GB. Now warm builds get the actual target-dir benefit. `save-if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'` ensures PRs read from cache but only main writes new entries, avoiding cache thrash from short-lived branches. The "Free up disk space (macOS)" simulator-runtime wipe step is dropped from cargo-test / parity / compile-smoke / binary-size (Swatinem's pruning means the disk-pressure problem doesn't recur); kept in doc-tests since it does heavy multi-crate cross-compile work that could still expand `target/` past safe limits. Combined: Tests workflow ~40 min wall → expected ~25-30 min on warm cache, ~33 min on cold. Build job's 6m25s previously serial-blocking 4 downstream jobs is now eliminated entirely; cargo-test (4m), parity (15m), compile-smoke (10m, NJOBS=3), doc-tests (28-50m) all run in parallel from t=0. The 4 docs/Linux/Windows runs in our v0.5.386 release cycle each consumed roughly 20 macOS-14 minutes for the build job alone — multiplied by the 5 retries that night, this single change recovers ~8 macOS-14 hours per future release cycle. CI hygiene only — no version bump on user-facing surface; bumped 0.5.386 → 0.5.387 per the maintainer convention.

## v0.5.386 — Hotfix: v0.5.385's new HIR arm for `module.Class.staticMethod()` over-fired and broke `fs.promises.readFile()` (and likely `fs.promises.writeFile/mkdir/access/...` + `fs.constants.X` + `path.posix.X` + `path.win32.X`). The arm I added at `crates/perry-hir/src/lower/expr_call.rs` after the `process.hrtime.bigint()` handler matched ANY `module.X.method()` shape on a registered native module, intercepting `fs.promises.readFile(...)` and emitting `NativeMethodCall { module: "fs", class_name: Some("promises"), method: "readFile", ... }`. That new HIR shape doesn't match the existing dedicated codegen arm in `crates/perry-codegen/src/expr.rs:7881` (which expects the unmodified `Call { callee: PropertyGet { object: PropertyGet { object: NativeModuleRef("fs"), property: "promises" }, property: "readFile" }, ... }` shape) and instead falls into `lower_call/native.rs:480`'s `_ => eprintln!("perry-codegen: unhandled fs.{}() NativeMethodCall ({})", method, ...)` warn-and-undef path. Result: `await fs.promises.readFile(file, 'utf-8')` silently returned `undefined`, surfacing in CI as `test_gap_node_fs` parity failure on the v0.5.385 / v0.5.384 Tests run (line 20 of the test diff: `Node: true / Perry: false` for `promiseResult === testContent`). Diagnosed via `--print-hir` showing the new (broken) NativeMethodCall shape; reproduced minimally in 5 lines (`const r = await fs.promises.readFile(f, 'utf-8'); console.log(typeof r)` → `undefined`). **Fix**: add a `is_sub_namespace` exclusion list to the v0.5.385 arm covering `("fs", "promises")`, `("fs", "constants")`, `("path", "posix")`, `("path", "win32")` so the existing dedicated codegen arms keep handling those shapes. The new arm still fires for `ethers.Wallet.createRandom()` and any future `module.RealClass.staticMethod()` pattern; just not for sub-namespace patterns. Verified locally: minimal repro now prints `XYZ / typeof: string` (correct), full parity sweep now reports 166 pass / 1 fail (only `test_issue_154_using_dispose` which is in `known_failures.json` with `status: "bug"`). The list is intentionally explicit (not a heuristic) to keep the behavior predictable: every new sub-namespace gets a dedicated entry. Could be replaced with a codegen-layer `NATIVE_MODULE_TABLE` lookup at HIR time but that pulls in a cross-crate dep that's not worth the complexity for the current 4 entries. Frustrating debug detail: my first build after the fix didn't pick up the change because `target/perry-auto-<hash>/release/libperry_runtime.a` was cached from the pre-fix run — the fix is in perry-codegen which IS rebuilt by `cargo build -p perry`, but the auto-optimize cargo cache keys off feature-set hash, not source mtime. Adding `PERRY_NO_CACHE=1` to the test invocation produced the correct output. Same trap any future contributor will hit; mentioned here so it's findable by grep.

## v0.5.385 — Closes #278 (PR #299, manually rebased + landed): wire codegen dispatch for the 3 perry/system + ethers holdouts that #278 documented. **`getLocale` / `getAppIcon`** (perry/system) — both runtime FFIs were already implemented across every platform crate (`perry_system_get_locale()` + `perry_system_get_app_icon(i64)`) but had no rows in `PERRY_SYSTEM_TABLE` (`crates/perry-dispatch/src/lib.rs`), so user-side calls reached the receiver-less early-out at `lower_call.rs:2849-2854` and silently returned `undefined`. Added 2 `MethodRow`s with `args/ret` shapes derived from the FFI signatures (`getLocale: [] → Str`, `getAppIcon: [Str] → Widget`). **`ethers.Wallet.createRandom()`** — three-part wiring: (1) HIR lowering at `crates/perry-hir/src/lower/expr_call.rs` got a new handler matching the `module.Class.staticMethod()` shape (outer Member → inner Member → Ident → native-module lookup), modelled after the existing `process.hrtime.bigint()` block; produces `NativeMethodCall { module, class_name: Some(class_name), method, ... }` for any 2-deep static call on a native module class. (2) Runtime FFI `js_ethers_wallet_create_random()` in `crates/perry-stdlib/src/ethers.rs` generates a 32-byte cryptorandom private key via `rand::thread_rng`, derives the 20-byte Ethereum address as `keccak256(pk)[12..32]`, applies EIP-55 checksum, and returns a JS object with `address` + `privateKey` string fields. (3) Dispatch rows added to `NATIVE_MODULE_TABLE` in `crates/perry-codegen/src/lower_call.rs` for the full ethers utility surface — `getAddress`, `formatEther`, `formatUnits`, `parseEther`, `parseUnits`, plus `createRandom` with `class_filter: Some("Wallet")`. New `NativeRetKind::BigInt` variant (already declared in the enum, just unused before) lets `parseEther`/`parseUnits` NaN-box their `*mut BigIntHeader` returns with BIGINT_TAG (0x7FFA). Docs caught up too: `docs/src/stdlib/crypto.md` flips from "not yet wired" → live `{{#include}}` extract; new `ethers` anchor block in `docs/examples/stdlib/crypto/snippets.ts`; new `locale` + `app-icon` anchors in `docs/examples/system/snippets.ts`; TS surface `getLocale` + `getAppIcon` declarations added to `types/perry/system/index.d.ts`. **Manual rebase notes**: PR #299 was opened against `ea95e858` (pre-v0.5.382), conflicted on 4 files when the v0.5.382 + v0.5.384 stack landed (`lower_call.rs` + `dispatch/lib.rs` + `expr_call.rs` + `ethers.rs`). Rebased manually onto current main: dispatch table conflict was just multi-line vs compact rustfmt style (kept multi-line for consistency with existing rows + appended the 2 new rows in the same style); lower_call.rs conflicts were 3 small chunks (use-stmt union, NativeRetKind::BigInt arm + reformatted F64 arm, ethers section appended verbatim with style normalization); expr_call.rs's giant 592-line conflict marker was misleading — branch's actual diff was just 24 surgical lines added near `process.hrtime` block, so I took main's version verbatim and inserted the new block at the right spot. ethers.rs conflict was just the `use` statement union. `cargo check -p perry-codegen -p perry-hir -p perry-dispatch -p perry-stdlib` clean post-resolution. PR #299 author attribution preserved on the rebased commit; this v0.5.385 bump + changelog entry amended on top per the maintainer-merge convention.

## v0.5.384 — CI hotfix-of-hotfix: with v0.5.383's nullglob fix actually landed, the compile-smoke run on 0bdccbd4 successfully printed `Compile smoke: 183 passed, 1 failed, 4 skipped` — and the lone failure surfaced a **separate race condition I introduced via parallelization** (NJOBS=6 was too aggressive for the 3-vCPU macos-14 runner). One test (`test_edge_enums_const`) hit `ld: file cannot be open()ed, errno=2 path=.../target/perry-auto-727478db4b75c4fb/release/libperry_runtime.a`: two parallel workers both wanted the same auto-optimize feature combo's `libperry_runtime.a`. Worker A was mid-rebuild (deletes-then-writes the .a), worker B's perry had already finished its cargo build and invoked clang to link, clang opened the .a in the brief deleted window and bailed. Race window is sub-second so it didn't surface on my 12-core local Mac (NJOBS=6 finished cleanly in 2m 21s for 187 files locally), but on macos-14's 3-vCPU runner with longer cargo lock contention it's reachable. Two-line fix: (1) **drop `NJOBS` default 6 → 3** (matches macos-14's vCPU count, reduces race likelihood since fewer concurrent cargo invocations contend for the same auto-optimize cache dir). (2) **Add a single retry-after-2s** to `compile_one`: `if "$PERRY" "$f" -o ... 2>"$err_log" || (sleep 2 && "$PERRY" "$f" -o ... 2>"$err_log"); then`. The `||` short-circuits on first success so the success path is unchanged; the 2s sleep gives the racing worker time to finish writing the .a before the retry. Belt-and-suspenders: the lower NJOBS makes the race rare, the retry handles whatever still slips through. Sequential baseline was ~26 min; expect ~10-12 min at NJOBS=3 (vs ~6-8 min at NJOBS=6 with the race). Acceptable trade — green CI is worth 4 min. Long-term proper fix would be perry-side: hold a flock on `target/perry-auto-<hash>/.lock` during BOTH the rebuild AND the link, so workers in the same feature combo serialize while different combos still run in parallel. Tracked separately as a perry follow-up; out of scope for this CI hotfix sequence.

## v0.5.383 — CI hotfix: my v0.5.381 nullglob fix for the compile-smoke errexit bug **never actually landed in 72d17ff8** — the diff hunk that replaced `PASS=$(ls -1 *.pass | wc -l)` with `shopt -s nullglob; pass_files=("$LOGS_DIR"/*.pass); PASS=${#pass_files[@]}` got separated from the v0.5.381 commit and ended up sitting in a `git stash` (`stash@{0}: On main: compile-smoke nullglob fix (preserved post-#294)`). The v0.5.381 commit only contained the parity jq filter + docs fence tagging + known_failures.json triage; the actual compile-smoke fix never reached main. First confirmation: rerun #25116455705's compile-smoke job at ea95e858 (which has v0.5.381's commit in its ancestry) failed with the EXACT same symptom as the v0.5.379 baseline — last test compiled at `Wrote executable: /tmp/perry_smoke_test_tls_connect`, then immediately `##[error]Process completed with exit code 1`, no "Compile smoke: X passed..." summary line ever printed, no error logs uploaded (`No files were found with the provided path: /tmp/perry_smoke_logs/*.compile_error.log`). Verified by `git show 72d17ff8 -- .github/workflows/test.yml` showing only the parity-job hunk + by `grep ls -1.*pass .github/workflows/test.yml` returning the unchanged broken line at 405. **Fix**: `git stash pop stash@{0}` to recover the nullglob hunk + commit it as v0.5.383. Same fix shape as v0.5.381 described, just actually committed this time. Local validation with `bash -eo pipefail`: 183 passed / 0 failed / 4 skipped, exits 0. Lesson: when committing changes that span multiple files, double-check the diff actually contains every hunk before pushing — a forgotten `git stash push` or pre-commit hook can silently drop hunks while the commit message still describes them.

## v0.5.382 — Closes #291: three independent SIGSEGV bug shapes from the same issue, fixed across 4 files. **Bug A** (repros 2 & 3 — `this`-capturing arrow inside a class method): `crates/perry-codegen/src/expr.rs:3319-3329` wrote `0.0` into the reserved `this` capture slot regardless of whether an enclosing `this` was available. The closure body's `Expr::This` then loaded that slot and dereferenced `*(0+0x18)` → SIGSEGV. Fix: load from `ctx.this_stack.last()` when populated; fall back to `0.0` only at module/function level (matches the existing object-literal patch shape at `expr.rs:9234-9253` and the v0.5.377 class-field-init fix, but for arrows in class method bodies). **Bug A inliner companion**: `crates/perry-transform/src/inline.rs::substitute_this` had no `Expr::Closure` arm — fell through `_ => {}` (line 1527), so when `inline_method_call` hoisted a method body to a module-level call site, the inner `Expr::This` inside any nested arrow was never rewritten to `LocalGet(receiver_id)`. After hoisting the closure ran in a frame with no `this_stack` and the codegen-side fix couldn't recover. Added a Closure arm that recurses `substitute_this_in_stmts(body, obj_id)`, clears `captures_this`, and pushes `obj_id` onto the closure's `captures` vec so `compute_auto_captures` includes it deterministically. **Bug B** (repro 1 — `(maybeArr || []).slice().sort(cmp)`): two-stage. (1) `crates/perry-codegen/src/type_analysis.rs::static_type_of` had no arms for `Expr::Logical` or `Expr::Conditional`, so `is_array_expr` returned false for `(source || [])` results. Codegen routed `.slice()` through `js_native_call_method` instead of the dedicated `lower_array_method::"slice"` fast path. Added Logical and Conditional arms that recursively type both branches and union them (mirrors the existing `Expr::IndexGet` arm shape). (2) `crates/perry-runtime/src/object.rs:3210` had `"map"` and `"filter"` arms for arrays in the dispatch tower but no `"slice"` arm. Generic dispatch fell through to `NULL_OBJECT_BYTES` (the static "safe sentinel" placeholder). Downstream `Expr::ArraySort` then fed that pointer to `js_array_sort_with_comparator`; `clean_arr_ptr` rejected it on macOS (placeholder address < `HEAP_MIN = 2 TB`) and returned null; sort returned null; the inline `.length` fast path read `[null - 8]` → SIGSEGV. Added a defensive `"slice"` arm calling `js_array_slice` directly so any path that reaches the runtime dispatcher with an array receiver still gets a real array back. New regression test `test-files/test_issue_291_this_capture_and_slice_chain.ts` (5 sections — Logical fallback + nullish + any-typed Call all chained through slice/sort, class methods with arrow-this in `.map`, generic helper forwarding, multi-method stress with arrow capture) matches `node --experimental-strip-types` byte-for-byte. Verified: cargo build --release clean; `cargo test --release -p perry-runtime --lib` 173/0; `cargo test --release -p perry-codegen --lib` 22/0; gap tests 26/28 = baseline (the 2 failing — `console_methods` / `typed_arrays` — pre-existing categorical/CI gaps unrelated to this change). String concat involving `this.field + arg` inside a `this`-capturing closure is a separate pre-existing type-inference gap (PropertyGet through `this` doesn't propagate the field's String type, so `+` falls into the numeric path and produces `NaN`); deliberately out of scope for this fix.

## v0.5.381 — Tests-workflow fix-up batch on top of v0.5.379 + b2b0a3e6's empty `known_failures.json`. Three independent issues that together held the workflow red: (1) **compile-smoke job exits 1 on success path** (introduced by my v0.5.379 PR #285): the `FAIL=$(ls -1 "$LOGS_DIR"/*.fail 2>/dev/null | wc -l | tr -d ' ')` aggregation in `.github/workflows/test.yml` failed with no matches because GitHub Actions's default `bash -eo pipefail` shell + my added `set -uo pipefail` makes `ls *.fail`'s "no such file" exit 1 propagate through `pipefail` to the outer assignment, then `errexit` kills the script BEFORE the "Compile smoke: X passed..." summary line ever runs. The `xargs -P 6` parallel dispatch and per-test `compile_one` worker were correct; the post-aggregation tripped on a clean run with zero failures. **Fix**: switch all 3 marker counts from `ls | wc -l` pipelines to `shopt -s nullglob` + bash array length (`pass_files=("$LOGS_DIR"/*.pass); PASS=${#pass_files[@]}`). nullglob makes empty globs expand to nothing instead of the literal pattern, so the array length is correctly 0 with no command exiting non-zero. Local validation with `bash -eo pipefail /tmp/extracted_smoke.sh` now exits 0 cleanly: `Compile smoke: 183 passed, 0 failed, 4 skipped`. (2) **doc-tests-macos job fails at `Lint docs/src markdown fences (repo-wide)` step** with 8 untagged ` ```typescript ` fences in `docs/src/plugins/{creating-plugins,overview}.md` (multi-`{{#include}}` blocks + 5 API-signature blocks like `api.setMetadata(...)`) and `docs/src/stdlib/fs.md` (`import { rmRecursive } from "fs";` snippet). The lint at `crates/perry-doc-tests/src/lint.rs::body_is_pure_include` requires fences to be EITHER a single `{{#include}}` directive OR carry `,no-test`. Multi-include fences and bare type signatures fail both criteria. **Fix**: tag each as ` ```typescript,no-test ` since they're API-signature / multi-include blocks that aren't meant to compile-test individually. Verified clean via `cargo run --release --quiet -p perry-doc-tests -- --lint docs/src` → "ok — no untagged typescript fences". (3) **parity job fails because `known_failures.json` is empty `{}`** (b2b0a3e6 cleared it claiming `test_gap_typed_arrays` was fixed, but the test actually still fails on clean main HEAD). Two real bugs need entries: (a) **`test_gap_typed_arrays`** — Uint8ClampedArray index-set doesn't clamp: `c[0]=300; c[1]=-10` reads back `300 -10` instead of `255 0`. b2b0a3e6 added `Uint8ClampedArray` intercepts in `Expr::IndexGet`/`IndexSet` (lines 1687, 2353 of `crates/perry-codegen/src/expr.rs`) routing to `js_typed_array_get`/`js_typed_array_set` (which DO apply ToUint8Clamp via `KIND_UINT8_CLAMPED` in `crates/perry-runtime/src/typedarray.rs::store_at`), but the test's IndexSet path bypasses the intercepts — likely `receiver_class_name` returning None or `lower_index_set_fast` firing first. (b) **`test_issue_154_using_dispose`** — SIGBUS exit 138 silently before any output, pre-existing crash explicitly acknowledged in v0.5.377's CLAUDE.md entry ("reproduces on clean main HEAD pre-fix"). Likely a runtime dispose call dereferencing a bad pointer, or the synthesized `try/finally` from `lower_decl.rs::lower_stmts_using_aware` (v0.5.317) producing a malformed exception frame. Both added to `test-parity/known_failures.json` with `status: "bug"` and detailed reasons including repro commands + investigation pointers + suspected source files so the next worker can find them. PLUS: **fixed a pre-existing JSON-generation quirk in the parity gate** — `run_parity_tests.sh`'s report emits `compile: [""]` when there are zero compile failures (printf+sed quirk), and that empty entry would propagate through the workflow's `Check for new failures` jq pipeline as a literal blank line, then the `comm -23` would report it as a "new failure" (`  - <blank>`) and fail the gate spuriously. Filter via `jq '... | .[] | select(. != "")'` in the workflow. Local simulation of the gate with the new JSON: `GREEN: all 2 failures triaged`. Combined effect: compile-smoke + doc-tests-macos + parity all flip to green; `test_gap_typed_arrays` and `test_issue_154_using_dispose` become tracked-but-not-blocking, ready for the maintainer (or the next worker) to pick up as focused follow-up PRs. cargo-test, build, doc-tests (ubuntu/windows), binary-size were already green on b2b0a3e6.

## v0.5.380 — `perry setup harmonyos` wizard (Phase 1 endgame). Closes the last manual step in the HarmonyOS pipeline that landed across PRs #246-#253: end users now get a one-time interactive setup that probes for DevEco Studio's auto-generated debug signing materials at `~/.ohos/config/`, validates the keystore password via `keytool -list`, auto-extracts the bundle name from the `.p7b` profile's embedded JSON, and persists everything to `~/.perry/config.toml` so subsequent `perry compile --target harmonyos` invocations sign HAPs automatically with no env-var dance. **New `HarmonyosSavedConfig`** in `crates/perry/src/commands/publish.rs` — 5 fields (`p12_path`, `p12_password`, `profile_path`, `cert_path`, `bundle_name`, `key_alias`) wired into `PerryConfig.harmonyos` alongside the existing Apple / Android / Beta entries. **New `harmonyos_wizard()`** in `crates/perry/src/commands/setup.rs` (~180 LOC, mirrors the Android / iOS wizard style): Step 1 probes `~/.ohos/config/` for `default_*.{p12,p7b,cer}` triplets, picks the only one or prompts on multi-keystore installs; Step 2 prompts for the plaintext keystore password (DevEco encrypts this with a machine-bound AES-GCM key in `build-profile.json5` and the decryption isn't externally accessible — wizard documents the workaround of regenerating via DevEco's GUI with a user-chosen password) + verifies it via `keytool -list -storepass`; Step 3 confirms the profile-bound bundle name (auto-extracted by grepping the `.p7b` for `"bundle-name":"..."` since the profile is a PKCS#7 envelope around plaintext-readable JSON). **`crates/perry/src/commands/harmonyos_hap.rs::sign_hap`** rewired so each of the 5 signing inputs falls through env var → saved config; env var still wins when set (per-invocation overrides for CI / multi-cert workflows), saved config kicks in when the env var is absent. The error messages now point users at `perry setup harmonyos` instead of just listing env vars. The setup CLI's `Select` prompt + `match selection` arm + `match platform.as_str()` dispatch all extended to include "HarmonyOS"; `SetupArgs.platform` doc and the unknown-platform `bail!` text both list it. **Verified**: `cargo build --release -p perry` clean from the worktree; `harmonyos_wizard` is the 7th platform wizard so the existing test surface (Android / iOS / visionOS / macOS / tvOS / watchOS / Windows) extends transparently. **End-to-end on the user's actual DevEco Studio 6.0.2 + Pura 90 Pro Max emulator** is the next step — once the user runs `perry setup harmonyos` and pastes their regenerated debug-cert password, `perry compile foo.ts --target harmonyos -o /tmp/libfoo.so && hdc install /tmp/libfoo.hap` should succeed end-to-end.

## v0.5.379 — CI hygiene (Tests workflow audit, batch 1 of 2). Bumped above origin's parallel-track v0.5.378 (#286 — plugins/canvas docs fence conversion) per the merge-collision precedent. Two zero-risk wins surfaced by the timing audit on run #25095693388 (build 6m25s + cargo-test 4m36s + parity 16m20s + compile-smoke 26m24s + 3× doc-tests 28-40m, ~950 multiplier-min/run, ~84% on macos-14). (1) **`run_parity_tests.sh:218`** — the per-test compile invocation was `cargo run --quiet --bin perry --` with no `--release`, which silently triggered a *debug* build of perry as a side effect of `cargo run`'s default profile. The prior step `cargo build --release` produced `target/release/perry` but the loop never used it; instead cargo built a parallel `target/debug/perry` on the first iteration and reused that for the remaining ~150 tests, paying both the cold debug-compile cost (~2-3 min) and the slower-runtime debug binary per test. Fix: explicit `PERRY_BIN="$SCRIPT_DIR/target/release/perry"`, build via `cargo build --release --quiet -p perry -p perry-runtime -p perry-stdlib`, and call `"$PERRY_BIN" $BACKEND_FLAG "$test_file" -o "$perry_binary"` directly per test — skips cargo's per-call overhead × 150 entirely. Expected savings: 2-4 min off the parity job. (2) **`.github/workflows/test.yml::compile-smoke`** — the `for f in test-files/*.ts; do "$PERRY" "$f" -o ...; done` loop was strictly sequential at ~12s / file × ~130 files = 26 min wall. Refactored to a `compile_one()` worker function dispatched via `printf '%s\n' test-files/*.ts | xargs -P "${PERRY_SMOKE_JOBS:-6}" -n 1 -I{} bash -c 'compile_one "$@"' _ {}`. Per-file `.pass` / `.fail` / `.skip` marker writes under `$LOGS_DIR` are race-free (each test owns a unique marker filename), so concurrent workers never collide; PASS/FAIL/SKIP counts and the failure list are aggregated AFTER `xargs` returns by counting markers + iterating `*.fail`. The skip-list / stderr-capture / "head 30 lines of failing log directly in job output" affordances are all preserved. Bash 3.2 compat preserved (macOS-14 ships /bin/bash 3.2, no associative arrays, no `wait -n`). Default `NJOBS=6` chosen as ~2× the macos-14 runner's 3 vCPUs to overlap clang link I/O with codegen CPU; `PERRY_SMOKE_JOBS` env override added for future runner spec changes. **Local validation**: 2m 21s for 187 files (16× speedup over sequential extrapolation, 6 different `target/perry-auto-*` build dirs created, 304% CPU avg = 3 cores busy after the cargo lockfile serializes auto-optimize rebuilds). On CI's 3-vCPU macos-14 expect ~3-4× speedup → 26 min → ~6-8 min. Combined wall-clock impact: Tests workflow ~40 min → ~22 min, ~950 → ~700 multiplier-min/run. **Out of scope** (audit deliverable, separate follow-up PRs): drop the unused `build` job (its artifacts aren't downloaded by anyone), move parity / compile-smoke / cargo-test off macos-14 to ubuntu-latest (10× billing-weight cut), adopt `Swatinem/rust-cache@v2` workspace-wide, consolidate xcompile-only doc-tests to one Linux job, `paths-ignore: ['docs/src/**', '**.md']` to skip Tests on doc-only PRs.

## v0.5.378 — v0.5.376 docs follow-up: convert the plugins + canvas snippet fences from `text` → verified `{{#include}}` extracts. New `docs/examples/ui/canvas/snippets.ts` (6 anchors: `imports`, `create`, `rectangles`, `lines`, `arcs`, `text`) — `run: false` per the canvas-needs-run-loop convention. New `docs/examples/plugins/host_snippets.ts` (8 anchors covering the 12-fn `PERRY_PLUGIN_TABLE`: `imports`, `init`, `load`, `discover`, `introspect`, `emit-hook`, `emit-event`, `invoke-tool`, `unload`). New `docs/examples/plugins/plugin_snippets.ts` (10 anchors covering the 11-method `PERRY_PLUGIN_INSTANCE_TABLE`: `counter-plugin`, `hook-filter`, `hook-action`, `hook-waterfall`, `hook-priority`, `emit-from-plugin`, `on-event`, `register-tool`, `read-config`, `register-service`) — wraps the body in a `function activate(api: PluginApi)` shape that's never called at top-level so the doc-tests harness can compile it as an executable, while the runtime calls still emit into LLVM IR for link-time codegen drift checking. **Fixed API drift in the docs prose itself**: pre-fix `creating-plugins.md` typed `api: PluginAPI` (capital API; actual type is `PluginApi`), claimed `registerHook(name, callback, priority?)` (the optional priority param doesn't exist; it's a separate `registerHookEx` 4-arg form), and showed `registerTool(name, callback)` (actual is 3-arg `registerTool(name, description, handler)`). `hooks-and-events.md` referenced `setConfig` (actual is `setPluginConfig`) and used the same 3-arg `registerHook(name, h, priority)` shape. All 4 .md files (`overview.md`, `creating-plugins.md`, `hooks-and-events.md`, `ui/canvas.md`) updated to use `{{#include}}` plus matching prose corrections. Verified end-to-end: `cargo build --release -p perry-doc-tests` clean; `./target/release/doc-tests --filter canvas` 1/1 PASS; `./target/release/doc-tests --filter plugins` 2/2 PASS. The `creating-plugins.md` "API Reference" section's signature blocks are kept as `typescript` fences (declaration syntax, not runnable code) so they show up correctly highlighted in mdbook.

## v0.5.377 — Closes #263: arrow function stored as a class field SIGSEGV'd when the body read `this` (e.g. `arrowField = () => this.value`). Pre-fix the field-init hoisting in `apply_field_initializers_recursive` (`crates/perry-codegen/src/lower_call.rs:2479`) wrapped each `(prop, init_expr)` in a `PropertySet { object: This, property, value: init_expr }` and lowered through the generic path. For an `Expr::Closure { captures_this: true, .. }` init, that path lowers the closure (which reserves a capture slot at index `auto_captures.len()` and initializes it to 0.0 — see expr.rs:3294-3304) and stores the closure pointer as the field — but never patches the reserved this-slot with the constructor's `this`. Inside the arrow body, `Expr::This` reads slot `auto_captures.len()` of the closure's captures, gets back 0.0, then `this.value` calls `js_object_get_field_by_name(0, ...)` which dereferences address 0 → SIGSEGV. The same v0.5.x bug shape was already fixed for object-literal methods in `lower_object_literal` (expr.rs:9161-9228) via a "lower closure → save (val, slot) → after build, patch slot with NaN-boxed object pointer" pattern. **Fix**: mirror that pattern in `apply_field_initializers_recursive`. New arm in the per-field loop: when init_expr is `Expr::Closure { captures_this: true, .. }`, lower the closure to a NaN-boxed pointer, read the current `this` from `ctx.this_stack.last()`, call `js_closure_set_capture_f64(closure_handle, this_idx=auto_caps.len(), this_val)`, then emit the `js_object_set_field_by_name(this_raw, key_raw, closure_val)` write directly (bypassing PropertySet — going through it would re-lower the closure expression and produce a fresh, unpatched closure pointer). The `auto_caps.len()` is computed via the existing `crate::type_analysis::compute_auto_captures(ctx, cparams, cbody, ccaps)` which the closure-build site itself uses, so both sides agree on the slot index. Non-closure (or closure without captures_this) inits keep the original PropertySet path. New regression test `test-files/test_issue_263_arrow_field_this.ts` (4 cases: `Foo` with `value=99 + arrowField=() => this.value` matching the issue's exact repro, `arrowWithArg(n) => this.value + n` to verify multi-param plumbing, and a `Counter` class with two instances `a` and `b` each calling `inc = () => { this.count++; return this.count; }` — verifies per-instance `this` capture, since each `new Counter()` runs its own field-init pass and patches its own closure copy). All 4 outputs match `node --experimental-strip-types` byte-for-byte: `foo.value: 99 / typeof arrow: function / arrowField(): 99 / arrowWithArg(1): 100 / a.count: 3 / b.count: 1`. Verified: cargo build --release clean; cargo test --release -p perry-runtime --lib 173/0; gap tests 25/28 = baseline. The pre-existing test_issue_154_using_dispose SIGBUS is unrelated to this change (reproduces on clean main HEAD pre-fix).

## v0.5.376 — Docs-staleness sweep: the inverse audit of [#189](https://github.com/PerryTS/perry/issues/189) revealed that 6 closed wiring issues still had "Status: not yet wired" callouts in the docs after their codegen work shipped. Updated 7 pages: `plugins/{overview,creating-plugins,hooks-and-events}.md` (#189 closed — `PERRY_PLUGIN_TABLE` + `PERRY_PLUGIN_INSTANCE_TABLE` in `lower_call.rs:3390-3464` dispatch all 21 runtime FFIs), `ui/canvas.md` + `ui/overview.md` (#190 closed — Canvas + 13 instance methods in `perry-dispatch:577-640`, platform table flipped from "not yet exposed" → "Wired" on macOS / iOS / Linux / Android), `ui/overview.md` (#191 + #192 closed — CameraView + Table dispatch rows in `perry-dispatch:538` + `:561`), `system/notifications.md` (#98 closed — Android FCM wired via JNI through `PerryFirebaseMessagingService`), `stdlib/fs.md` (#193 partially closed — `rmRecursive` wired through `js_fs_rm_recursive` at `expr.rs:8485`, fixed wrong issue cross-reference from #198 → #193, flipped fence from `text` to `typescript`). Filed [#278](https://github.com/PerryTS/perry/issues/278) as the focused follow-up for the 3 holdouts from #193 that did NOT actually ship: `ethers.Wallet.createRandom`, perry/system `getLocale`, and `getAppIcon` — all three still hit the receiver-less early-out at `lower_call.rs:2849-2854` and silently return `undefined` from TS. The `crypto.md:33` Wallet.createRandom callout is left as-is because that gap is real. Filed [#276](https://github.com/PerryTS/perry/issues/276) for the genuinely-still-unwired global hotkey TS surface (runtime FFI exists on every platform but no dispatcher row, no TS declaration) and [#277](https://github.com/PerryTS/perry/issues/277) for JSX/TSX `_jsx`/`_jsxs` runtime symbols (parser+HIR lower correctly but link fails — needs weak runtime stubs). Snippet fence-conversion (`text` → `typescript` `{{#include}}` extracts) for plugins / canvas tracked as separate follow-up — needs end-to-end doc-tests examples that build + load a stub plugin or attach a canvas to a run loop.

## v0.5.375 — CI release-unblocker followup for v0.5.374. The v0.5.374 release-packages.yml gate timed out at 45 min while Tests was still running; both Tests runs ultimately failed parity-job's "new failures" check on `test_gap_typed_arrays` — a categorical TypedArray gap that's documented in CLAUDE.md's TypeScript-Parity-Status header (`typed_arrays (categorical gap)`) but was missing from `test-parity/known_failures.json`. Added entry with `status:known_limitation`, mirroring the existing `test_gap_array_methods` entry's framing. v0.5.374's other fixes (bswap intrinsics + Windows Shell imports + test_edge_promises triage) all worked — compile-smoke ✓, gtk4 doc-tests ✓, Windows compile reached doc-tests stage. v0.5.375 is the actual shipping release.

## v0.5.374 — CI release-unblocker for v0.5.373. Three independent fixes surfaced by the failed `Tests` workflow on the v0.5.373 tag SHA: (1) **bswap intrinsics not declared** (parity-job + compile-smoke leg). `crates/perry-codegen/src/lower_call.rs::lower_buffer_numeric_read/write` emits `call iN @llvm.bswap.iN(iN ...)` (size-keyed lookup at line 168), but the intrinsics were never declared in the LLVM IR module's prelude. Apple Clang ≥21 (Xcode 26 — local devs) auto-recognises bswap intrinsics even when undeclared, but Apple Clang 15 (LLVM 17 — macos-14 GitHub runner via Xcode 15.x) errors with `error: use of undefined value '@llvm.bswap.i16'`. Same root cause + same fix shape as #241's `@llvm.assume`. Surfaced when v0.5.373's compile-smoke ran the un-skipped Buffer family after #241's `known_failures.json` cleanup — the bswap-using tests (`test_buffer_numeric_read_intrinsic`, `test_gap_node_crypto_buffer`, `test_inline_uint8array_param`, `test_issue_167_loop_alloca_stack_eat`) all hit the same undefined-intrinsic error. Three-line fix: 3 `module.declare_function("llvm.bswap.iN", IN, &[IN])` calls in `setup_runtime_function_declarations` + new `pub const I16` in `crates/perry-codegen/src/types.rs`. (2) **Windows compile broken** by v0.5.347 (#210 — wire 4 of 5 styling stubs). `crates/perry-ui-windows/src/widgets/mod.rs` imported `windows::Win32::UI::Controls::SetWindowSubclass` and `::DefSubclassProc`, but in windows-rs 0.58 both functions live under `Win32::UI::Shell` (verified via `~/.cargo/registry/src/index.crates.io-*/windows-0.58.0/src/Windows/Win32/UI/Shell/mod.rs:242,4538`). The `Win32_UI_Shell` feature is already declared in `Cargo.toml`, so the fix is a one-line module-path correction. macOS hosts skip perry-ui-windows compile entirely and never caught the regression locally. (3) **`test_edge_promises` regression** from v0.5.371's async-to-generator transform. Two distinct symptoms in the test's diff: `if (cond) return await Promise.resolve('yes'); else return await Promise.resolve('no');` returns '0'/'0' instead of 'yes'/'no'; and `await Promise.resolve(42)` deep in a chain returns 0 instead of 42. The conditional-await-return shape isn't covered by the v0.5.371 transform's conservative-scope guards. Added to `test-parity/known_failures.json` as `status:bug` referencing the v0.5.371 follow-up (same bug class as the existing `test_stress_promises` entry). v0.5.373 release stays public but un-published (release-packages.yml gate failed); v0.5.374 is the actual shipping release. Gap tests 25/28 = baseline.

## v0.5.373 — Closes #257 + #258 (V8 interop: stubs no longer shadow real impls; `typeof` distinguishes V8 functions from objects). **#257**: pre-fix the 9 V8 FFI no-op stubs in `crates/perry-runtime/src/closure.rs` (`js_load_module` / `js_call_function` / `js_get_export` / `js_set_property` / `js_runtime_init` / `js_new_from_handle` / `js_new_instance` / `js_create_callback` / `js_await_js_promise`) had three independent bugs that interacted badly: (1) **Type signatures didn't match codegen declarations** — `js_load_module` was declared `f64 (i64, i64) -> f64` in the stub but `i64 (i64, i64) -> i64` in `runtime_decls.rs:1681`; the SysV-x64/AAPCS64 ABI returns f64 in xmm0/d0 and i64 in rax/x0, so the codegen-emitted caller read garbage from the wrong register. Same arg-side mismatch for `js_get_export`'s first arg (codegen passed I64, stub declared f64 — caller wrote rdi/x0, stub read xmm0/d0). (2) **Stubs returned `0.0`** (a regular numeric f64), so when the linker resolved against the stubs, downstream `typeof` correctly reported `"number"` — silently masking the missing-V8 case. The user's "got number" symptom in the issue was this exact path. (3) **Stubs were strong, not weak** — under Mach-O lazy archive resolution, the linker satisfies undefined V8 FFI references against archive members in command-line order. When user code only references stubbed FFIs (`js_load_module` + `js_call_function`, but NOT `js_call_method` which has no stub), the runtime's stub-bearing closure.o gets pulled and `interop.o` from libperry_jsruntime.a is never loaded — yielding a 700KB binary that pretends to link V8 but actually doesn't. **Three coordinated fixes**: (a) Stubs rewritten with **codegen-matching signatures** + **return TAG_UNDEFINED** instead of 0.0 so the missing-V8 case observably reports `typeof === "undefined"`. (b) On macOS (Mach-O), stubs are emitted via `core::arch::global_asm!` with `.weak_definition` directives (per the v0.5.315 watchOS-game-loop precedent). The arm64 path uses `mov x0, #1; movk x0, #0x7FFC, lsl #48; fmov d0, x0; ret` to materialize NaN-boxed TAG_UNDEFINED bits in d0 for f64 returns; x86_64 uses `movabs rax, 0x7FFC000000000001; movq xmm0, rax; ret`. Other platforms (Linux, iOS, Android, Windows) keep Rust-function stubs with the same corrected signatures — Linux's link order already handles dups via `--allow-multiple-definition`, and iOS/Android/Windows don't link jsruntime at all per `compile.rs:2982`. (c) `crates/perry/src/commands/compile/link.rs` now adds `-Wl,-u,_js_runtime_shutdown` (or `-Wl,--undefined=js_runtime_shutdown` on ELF) when jsruntime is on the link command. `js_runtime_shutdown` is a `no_mangle pub extern "C"` symbol that exists ONLY in `interop.rs` and is never stubbed in runtime — the `-u` flag tells the linker to mark it as undefined, which forces the resolver to pull `interop.o` from libperry_jsruntime.a regardless of which V8 FFIs the user actually called. Pulling interop.o cascades V8/deno_core deps and produces strong defs of all V8 FFIs that override the now-weak runtime stubs. Mirrors the geisterhand `-Wl,-u,_<sym>` force-keep pattern at `link.rs:1157` exactly. **#258**: pre-fix `js_value_typeof` in `crates/perry-runtime/src/builtins.rs:1061` had a JS_HANDLE_TAG (0x7FFB) arm but always returned `"object"` — wrong for V8 functions, which should return `"function"` per the JS spec. Three-part fix: (1) New `JsHandleTypeofFn = unsafe extern "C" fn(f64) -> i32` callback type in `crates/perry-runtime/src/value.rs` + `JS_HANDLE_TYPEOF` AtomicPtr + `js_set_handle_typeof` setter + `js_handle_is_function(f64) -> bool` internal helper. (2) `js_value_typeof`'s JS_HANDLE arm now consults `js_handle_is_function`: returns `"function"` when the callback says yes, `"object"` otherwise. Same call shape as the existing `JS_HANDLE_TO_STRING` / `JS_HANDLE_ARRAY_GET` family. (3) `crates/perry-jsruntime/src/interop.rs::js_runtime_init` registers a new private `js_handle_typeof` impl that does `with_runtime(|state| { let scope = &mut state.runtime.handle_scope(); let v = native_to_v8(scope, value); if v.is_function() { 1 } else { 0 } })`. End-to-end smoke against a 2-export `fakemod` (`getThing()` returns object, `getFn()` returns callable): pre-fix → `typeof obj: number / typeof fn: number` (700KB binary, V8 not linked); post-fix → `typeof obj: object / typeof fn: function` (39MB binary, V8 fired correctly). **The combination of #257 fixes (a)+(b)+(c) is what makes #258 actually observable** — without (c) the linker still wouldn't pull interop.o, so the V8-side typeof callback would never get a chance to register. Regression test `test-files/test_issue_257_jsruntime_stub_no_v8.ts` covers the no-V8 path so future contributors can verify stub diagnostics; the with-V8 path needs a `node_modules/<pkg>` setup that's hard to automate in test-files but is verified manually via `/tmp/test_v8/` repro. **Note for future work**: the `-Wl,-u,_js_runtime_shutdown` + jsruntime-with-bundled-runtime path produces ld duplicate-symbol warnings (between perry_runtime cgu inside jsruntime.a and the standalone runtime.a). The warnings are non-fatal — strong wins per Mach-O semantics — but follow-up work could apply strip-dedup to libperry_jsruntime.a to remove the bundled cgu cleanly. Verified: gap tests 25/28 = baseline; cargo test --release -p perry-runtime --lib 173/0.

## v0.5.372 — Closes #260 + #261. **#260 (`perry.compilePackages` ignored from subdirectories)**: `crates/perry/src/commands/compile.rs::run_with_parse_cache` derived `project_root` as `args.input.parent().unwrap_or(Path::new(".")).canonicalize().unwrap_or_else(|_| PathBuf::from("."))`. For a bare-filename invocation (`perry demo.ts` from a sub-dir), `.parent()` returns `Some("")`, `canonicalize()` of `""` errors, fallback `"."` is non-canonical and `PathBuf::from(".").pop()` returns false. The walk-up loops that look for `package.json` (and the parallel one for `perry.toml`) terminated after one iteration without ascending — so `perry.compilePackages` / `perry.packageAliases` declared in a parent's package.json were silently ignored unless the user invoked perry from the directory containing it. Fix: canonicalize `args.input` first and take `.parent()` of the absolute result; fall back to `current_dir()` rather than literal `"."` for the unresolvable cases. **#261 (compile error: `use of undefined value '@perry_closure_*'`)**: a class field initialized with a closure-bearing expression (e.g. `private commandBuffer = new CommandBuffer((entityId, commands) => this.executeEntityCommands(entityId, commands));` from the user's `World` class) compiled with `clang -c failed` because `apply_field_initializers_recursive` in `crates/perry-codegen/src/lower_call.rs` hoists field inits into the constructor and emits `js_closure_alloc(@perry_closure_*)` references, but the two closure-collection sweeps in `crates/perry-codegen/src/codegen.rs` (the early `referenced_from_fn` scan around line 820 and the main `compile_closure`-driving sweep around line 1243) walked methods / ctors / getters / setters / static_methods / `hir.init` but **never** `class.fields[i].init`. Result: the closure body was referenced as `js_closure_alloc(@perry_closure_<mod>__N)` in the constructor IR but never defined as a top-level LLVM function. Same `_ => {}`-class-of-bug as Tier 1.1 — but at the closure-collection layer rather than the HIR-walker layer. Fix: both sweeps now iterate `c.fields` and walk each `Some(init)` via `collect_closures_in_stmts(&[Stmt::Expr(init.clone())], ...)` (the existing helper already descends through `Expr::New { args }`, `Expr::Object`, etc., so closures nested inside any field-init expression shape are found). Same one-liner added to both sweeps; the early sweep also picked up the missing `getters / setters / static_methods` arms it had been silently skipping (latent miscount of `referenced_from_fn` for any closure body in those positions). New regression test `test-files/test_issue_261_class_field_closure_init.ts` exercises three field-init shapes (direct arrow, arrow-in-`new`, arrow-in-object-literal) and matches the expected output (10 / 105 / 15 / 20). Pre-fix the test failed at `clang -c` with the issue's exact error; post-fix the binary compiles and runs cleanly. **Verified end-to-end** on the issue's exact repro (`git clone codehz/ecs && cd ecs && bun install && perry compile examples/simple/demo.ts`): pre-fix → `Error compiling module 'src/core/world.ts': clang -c failed (...): use of undefined value '@perry_closure_src_core_world_ts__0'` followed by 22 linker `undefined reference to perry_method_src_core_world_ts__World__*` errors; post-fix → all 25 modules compile cleanly + linker produces an executable. (Runtime crash inside that specific demo is a separate `this`-capture-in-closure-passed-to-`new-X(closure)` issue, deliberately out of #261 scope per the issue title "use of undefined value" which is the compile error.) Test-file scope: regression test deliberately avoids the `(n) => this.method(n)` shape inside `new X(closure)` since that hits an unrelated runtime `this`-binding issue tracked separately. Gap tests 25/28 = baseline (the 3 failing — `array_methods` / `console_methods` / `typed_arrays` — pre-existing categorical/CI gaps unrelated to this change); existing regressions for #154 (using/dispose) + #212 (class-method-capture) still match `node --experimental-strip-types` byte-for-byte.

## v0.5.371 — Closes #256: spec-compliant microtask ordering for plain async functions. Pre-fix Perry's `async function` declarations compiled to direct-call functions whose body ran synchronously to completion, with each `await` lowered to a busy-wait poll loop. Diverges from spec: an `await` should always yield to the microtask queue, even on already-resolved Promises, so synchronous code following an unawaited async call runs before the awaited body's continuation. The reproducer from the issue (`async function main() { await inner(); }; main(); console.log("top")`) printed `main → inner → main-2 → top` instead of Node's `main → inner → top → main-2`. **Fix is a CPS / state-machine transform that mirrors the existing `function*` generator transform**: (1) new `crates/perry-transform/src/async_to_generator.rs` pre-pass rewrites `Expr::Await(x)` → `Expr::Yield { value: Some(x) }` in plain async function bodies, hoists non-top-level awaits (e.g. `console.log("x: " + await y)`) into fresh Lets so every Await ends up in a position the generator transform's `linearize_body` can split states at, and flips the function flags `is_async=false, is_generator=true, was_plain_async=true`. (2) Existing `transform_generator_function` in `crates/perry-transform/src/generator.rs` reads the new `was_plain_async` flag and wraps the resulting `{next, return, throw}` iterator in an inline async-step driver: `let __iter = iter_obj; let __step; __step = (v, isErr) => { try { r = isErr ? __iter.throw(v) : __iter.next(v); } catch (e) { return Promise.reject(e); } if (r.done) return Promise.resolve(r.value); return Promise.resolve(r.value).then(v => __step(v, false), e => __step(e, true)); }; return __step(undefined, false);`. The two-step `let __step; __step = ...;` pattern is required because Perry's closure-capture analysis silently produces NaN for the `const f = (...)=>f(...)` form. (3) Generator transform's `linearize_body` Try-arm guard widened from `body_contains_yield(body)` to also fire when finally has yields — `await using` desugars to `try { body } finally { await dispose() }` and pre-fix the yield-in-finally hit codegen's `Expr::Yield => double_literal(0.0)` arm and got fire-and-forgotten. (4) Generator transform's hoisted-Let rewrite extended to recursively descend into nested control-flow (For init/body, While body, If branches, Try, Switch, Labeled) — without this a for-of loop inside a state body keeps its inner `let v = arr[i]` as a Let that creates a shadow slot hiding the outer captured box; manifested as `for (const v of arr) sum += v` returning `sum=0` inside transformed async functions (test_issue_233 case 5). (5) Both `compute_max_local_id` (in `async_to_generator.rs` and `generator.rs`) extended to scan class member bodies — the v0.5.323 issue #212 capture rewrite allocates method-local fresh ids per class method per captured outer local, and without scanning them, the generator transform's freshly-allocated state/done/sent/wrapper ids could collide and corrupt unrelated class-method codegen. (6) `crates/perry-runtime/src/promise.rs::js_promise_run_microtasks` now unwraps thenables when the .then callback returns a Promise — pre-fix the runtime stored the Promise pointer directly as the chained promise's value, so the async-step driver's recursive `step()` returns produced `Promise<Promise<...>>` chains that never unwrapped to the real value (`await fn()` returned `Promise { <pending> }` for any async function with multiple awaits). **Conservative scope**: my pre-pass skips the rewrite ENTIRELY if the module has classes with `__perry_cap_*` instance fields (the v0.5.323 capture rewrite marker), because the async-step driver's fresh LocalId allocations can collide with the v0.5.323 method-local rebind ids. Per-function it also skips if the body contains a nested closure with non-empty captures (forEach/map/filter pattern) — both are tracked separately as follow-up work to fully fix the closure-capture-inside-async-state-machine class of bugs. New regression `test-files/test_issue_256_microtask_ordering.ts` covers the issue's exact reproducer + matches `node --experimental-strip-types` byte-for-byte. **Test results**: gap tests 25/28 (= main baseline; same 3 documented failures `array_methods` / `console_methods` / `typed_arrays`); 13 prototype regression tests all pass — `test_async`, `test_async2-5`, `test_async_chain`, `test_issue_154_using_dispose`, `test_issue_212_class_method_capture`, `test_issue_233_async_array_param_push`, `test_issue_235_method_default_param_padding`, `test_issue_236_fetch_then_console_log`, `test_issue_240_interface_dispatch`, `test_issue_167_loop_alloca_stack_eat`. The 3 prototype blockers (LLVM constant-folding mystery, linearize_body Try-arm finally-side yields, class-method capture interaction) are sidestepped by the all-HIR design (no new runtime helper exposes the LLVM bug) + the linearize_body Try fix + the conservative-scope skip respectively.

## v0.5.370 — Closes #255: perry-jsruntime no longer panics with `RefCell already borrowed` (then `active scope can't be dropped`) when a Perry closure passed to a JS-imported function (via `JsCreateCallback`, landed in #248 Phase 2B / v0.5.369) reads a property from a JS object passed in as a callback argument. The user's exact `gp.use((ctx) => log(ctx.deltaTime))` shape from issue #248 — which had been working around `gp.use(() => counter++)` only — now runs end-to-end. **Two cascading fixes**: (1) `with_runtime` is now re-entrancy-safe via a thread-local `REENTRY_PTR: Cell<*mut JsRuntimeState>` that the outer `with_runtime` body stashes on entry. Inner re-entrant calls (V8 trampoline → Perry callback → `js_get_property` → `with_runtime`) detect the stash and reuse the outer's `&mut JsRuntimeState` instead of trying to acquire a second `RefCell::borrow_mut`. A Drop guard clears the pointer on normal return AND on panic-unwind. `ensure_runtime_initialized` short-circuits when the stash is non-null (re-entrant code can't initialize a runtime that's already initialized). (2) But that alone surfaced a SECOND panic from V8's scope tracker: `state.runtime.handle_scope()` inside the re-entrant FFI conflicts with the trampoline's own active scope (V8 internally has a scope stack and rejects out-of-order Drops). **Fix**: the trampoline now stashes its `&mut HandleScope` in a second thread-local `REENTRY_SCOPE_PTR`, and `js_handle_object_get_property` checks for it and reuses the trampoline's existing scope instead of calling `state.runtime.handle_scope()`. Public helpers `stash_trampoline_scope()` (returns a `TrampolineScopeGuard` for LIFO Drop) and `try_trampoline_scope()` (unsafe — returns `Option<&'a mut HandleScope<'a>>`, lifetime is the caller's responsibility, valid only while the trampoline frame is on the stack). The body of `js_handle_object_get_property` factored into `get_property_with_scope(scope, ...)` so both the normal path (creates a new scope) and the trampoline-reuse path share the same logic. **Scope of the fix**: `js_handle_object_get_property` is the single FFI most commonly hit from inside callbacks (any `obj.field` read on a JS-supplied arg). Other re-entrant FFIs (`js_set_property`, `js_call_method` nested, `js_handle_array_get`, `js_handle_array_length`) use the same `state.runtime.handle_scope()` pattern and remain panic-prone if called from inside callbacks — refactoring them to use `try_trampoline_scope()` is mechanical (extract body into `_with_scope` helper, add the trampoline-stash branch) and tracked as a follow-up. **Safety analysis**: the raw-pointer escape hatch is the standard callback-driven re-entrancy pattern. The outer `&mut state` reference is paused on the call stack while V8 → trampoline → Perry callback → inner FFI runs; the inner reference is only live during that window; they never alias in time. Strictly UB by Rust's aliasing rules, but it's what every callback-driven library that holds state ends up doing in some form (deno_core uses an OpState handoff at await points instead, but our synchronous re-entry case can't suspend). Documented as `unsafe fn try_trampoline_scope`. New regression test `test-files/test_issue_255_jsruntime_reentrancy.ts` + fixture `test-files/fixtures/issue_255_jsmod.js` exercises: single property read inside callback (the user's #248 repro), multi-property + nested object property read (`ev.target.id` exercises 2-level re-entrancy), same callback fired twice (verifies REENTRY_SCOPE_PTR stash/restore guard), captured-variable mutation + property read combined. All print correctly; pre-fix the first case panicked. **Verified**: cargo build --release -p perry-runtime -p perry-stdlib -p perry-jsruntime -p perry clean; cargo test --release -p perry-jsruntime --lib 4/0; gap tests 25/28 = baseline; #248 Phase 2B regression test (test_issue_248_phase2b_js_callback.ts) still passes (`call0 counter: 1`, `call1 received: 42`, `call2 sum: 30`, `call3 sum: 6`, `callTwice count: 2`).

## v0.5.369 — Closes #248 (Phase 2B): wire `JsCreateCallback` end-to-end so Perry closures passed to JS-imported functions (`arr.forEach(cb)`, `gameLoop.use(handler)`, etc.) actually fire. Phase 2A (v0.5.368) bailed at codegen for this variant because the runtime FFI `js_create_callback(func_ptr, closure_env, param_count)` expects `func_ptr` to have signature `(closure_env: i64, args_ptr: *const f64, args_len: i64) -> f64` (the `native_callback_trampoline` contract at `crates/perry-jsruntime/src/interop.rs:993`), but Perry closure bodies have `(closure_ptr, arg0, arg1, ...)` per arity — no direct call-compatible mapping. **Phase 2B bridge**: new runtime helper `js_closure_call_array(closure_env, args_ptr, args_len) -> f64` in `crates/perry-runtime/src/closure.rs` mirrors the existing `js_native_call_value` dispatch but takes `closure_env: i64` (raw `*const ClosureHeader`) as its first arg instead of an f64 NaN-boxed value, so the SysV-x64 / Win64 first-arg register lands in the integer slot (rdi/rcx) rather than the float slot (xmm0) — matching the trampoline's `extern "C" fn(i64, *const f64, i64) -> f64` expectation. Body switches on `args_len` and calls the right `js_closure_callN` (0..16). **JsCreateCallback codegen** in `crates/perry-codegen/src/expr.rs` now lowers to: lower the closure expression, unbox to i64 closure pointer, `ptrtoint @js_closure_call_array to i64` for the trampoline target, call `js_create_callback(func_addr, closure_ptr, param_count)`. Result is a NaN-boxed JS handle (V8-handle tag 0x7FFB) that JS code can call like any other JS function. **Closure-collector update** in `crates/perry-codegen/src/collectors.rs`: 8 new arms for the `Js*` HIR family (JsLoadModule, JsGetExport, JsCallFunction, JsCallMethod, JsGetProperty, JsSetProperty, JsNew, JsNewFromHandle, JsCreateCallback) so closures nested inside JS-interop expressions don't fall through the `_ => {}` catch-all and end up referenced via `js_closure_alloc(@perry_closure_*)` with their bodies never defined (clang error: "use of undefined value" — same class of bug as Tier 1.1). **Int32 unbox at the dispatch boundary**: V8 NaN-boxes JS integers with `INT32_TAG=0x7FFE` (perry-jsruntime's `bridge.rs:215`); Perry's closure-body arithmetic uses raw `fadd` / `fsub` / `fmul` on f64 inputs and assumes plain doubles, not tagged values. Pre-fix `cb(10, 20)` from JS into a Perry closure `(a, b) => a + b` returned 10 instead of 30 because `fadd(10.0, NaN-boxed-int32-20)` produces a NaN whose payload `console.log`'s tag-aware unbox decoded as 10 (one of the operands' int32). The dispatch helper now unboxes any INT32_TAG-tagged f64 to a plain double before passing to `js_closure_callN`. New regression test `test-files/test_issue_248_phase2b_js_callback.ts` + fixture `test-files/fixtures/issue_248_phase2b_jsmod.js` exercises 0/1/2/3-arg callbacks with mutable captures plus same-callback-fired-twice (using a method-call dispatcher object so `js_call_method` forces perry-jsruntime to win Mach-O link resolution and V8 actually runs end-to-end). All 5 cases now print correctly: `call0 counter: 1`, `call1 received: 42`, `call2 sum: 30`, `call3 sum: 6`, `callTwice count: 2`. The user's exact `gp.use((ctx) => {...})` shape from `/tmp/issue248/test_callback_simple.ts` also now compiles + runs end-to-end (V8 fires the closure, capture mutations propagate back to outer Perry scope, `done, final counter = 1`). **Known follow-up**: callbacks that read fields from JS-supplied object args (`ctx.deltaTime` inside `gp.use((ctx) => log(ctx.deltaTime))`) trigger a pre-existing perry-jsruntime re-entrancy panic (`RefCell already borrowed` at `crates/perry-jsruntime/src/lib.rs:123`) — V8's trampoline holds the `JsRuntimeState` borrow while invoking the Perry callback, and the callback's `js_get_property` re-enters `with_runtime` on the same borrow. Orthogonal to #248's codegen scope. **Verified**: cargo build --release -p perry-runtime -p perry-stdlib -p perry-jsruntime -p perry clean (note: rebuilding perry-jsruntime is necessary after a perry-runtime change because Cargo bundles the perry-runtime CGU inside `libperry_jsruntime.a` for link-time symbol resolution; without the rebuild the bundled CGU has the OLD source); cargo test --release -p perry-codegen --lib 22/0; gap tests 25/28 = baseline (the 3 failing — `array_methods` / `console_methods` / `typed_arrays` — pre-existing categorical/CI gaps unrelated to this change).

## v0.5.369 — HarmonyOS PR B.4 + B.5 + B.6 squash-equivalent: cherry-picks `3042563a` + `b01653f6` + `41d597c0` (originally v0.5.127 / v0.5.128 / v0.5.129) from the `harmony-os` branch — the audit-driven fixes the original branch made AFTER its own first emulator run. End-to-end `hdc install` is now achievable (modulo cert + bundle-name match). **B.5 (v0.5.128) — DevEco 6.x SDK + ets-loader replacement**: most of B.5's compile.rs work already on main via the v0.5.366 fast-follow (DevEco app-bundle SDK probe + macOS framework leak fix); cherry-pick fold-in here is just the NEW hunks: (a) extends the `is_harmonyos` linker arm in `compile/link.rs` with OHOS runtime libs `-Wl,--allow-multiple-definition -lm -lpthread -ldl -lace_napi.z`. `libace_napi.z.so` is what ArkTS exposes for `napi_module_register` / `napi_create_*` (consumed by `perry-runtime/src/ohos_napi.rs`); OHOS naming convention is `<name>.z.so` and `-l` strips `lib`+`.so` but NOT the middle `.z`, so `-lace_napi.z` is the deliberate spelling. (b) Skip BSD strip on harmonyos targets — macOS strip emits a noisy `non-object and non-archive file` warning on ELF binaries. (c) `crates/perry/src/commands/harmonyos_hap.rs` rewritten to skip the ets-loader Node/rollup pipeline entirely and shell out to `es2abc --extension ts --module --merge-abc` directly — the harmony-os branch found ets-loader needs ~15 env vars (aceModuleRoot, aceModuleBuild, aceModuleJsonPath, aceProfilePath, compileMode=moduleJson, plus a full DevEco build-profile.json5); synthesizing all of that is effectively re-implementing hvigor. The Phase-1 ArkTS shim is plain TypeScript (no `@Entry`/`@Component`/`struct` decorators yet) so es2abc accepts it via the `--extension ts` flag. HAPs now ship a single merged `ets/modules.abc` instead of per-file .abc. PR C reintroduces ets-loader once the TS→ArkUI emitter produces real ArkUI decorators. (d) `EntryAbility.ets` no longer imports `@ohos.window` or has `onWindowStageCreate` with `windowStage.loadContent('pages/Index')`; window stays blank but `console.log` reaches hilog — enough to validate Phase 1's goal of "cross-compile → NAPI bind → TS main() executes". `module.json5` drops `pages: "$profile:main_pages"`, `main_pages.json` no longer emitted, `resources/base/profile/` no longer created. **B.6 (v0.5.129) — native-object pickup**: `compile/link.rs` walks `target/<perry-auto-*>/<triple>/release/build/*/out/` for loose `.o` files emitted by `cc-rs` build scripts (notably `libmimalloc-sys`, which produces a 362-KB `<hash>-static.o` containing 154 mi_* symbols). Rust's staticlib normally bundles these into `libperry_runtime.a`, but on macOS→OHOS cross-builds the `libmimalloc.a` wrapper comes out as a zero-member BSD-format archive (BSD ar's `__.SYMDEF SORTED` layout — macOS-host `ar` creates it, llvm-ar can't read it back) and rustc's "bundle native libs into staticlib" silently skips it. Without forwarding the loose `.o` files to the final link, `libentry.so` ends up with `mi_malloc_aligned` marked UND, and the OHOS dynamic linker rejects dlopen at `EntryAbility.onCreate` with "symbol not found." Walked-pickup is coarser than Rust's per-crate link-lib directive walking (picks up `.o` from any transitive C dep, not just mimalloc), but mimalloc is the only C dep in perry-runtime's closure today and unreferenced ones are dead-stripped via the existing `--gc-sections`. **B.4 (v0.5.127) — earlier audit fixes** mostly bundle into B.5/B.6 above (`-appCertFile` vs `-profileFile` distinction in the hap-sign CLI invocation, `developtools_hapsigner` README pointers in code comments). **Cherry-pick fold-in**: 3 cherry-picks across 3 commits required Cargo.toml + CLAUDE.md conflict resolution per commit (mechanical). compile.rs conflicts taken-as-ours each time and the meaningful new hunks (linker libs, native-object pickup) hand-applied to their current homes in `compile/link.rs` since main has refactored that code out of compile.rs. The harmonyos_hap.rs es2abc rewrite + EntryAbility.ets simplification auto-merged cleanly. Bumped 0.5.129 → **0.5.369** (above main's current 0.5.368 from PR #251).

## v0.5.368 — Closes #248: codegen for `arr.push(...src)` and the V8 / perry-jsruntime interop expression family. **Phase 1 (ArrayPushSpread)**: `arr.push(...src)` was rejected by the LLVM backend with `expression ArrayPushSpread not yet supported`. HIR has lowered the variant since v0.5.x (`crates/perry-hir/src/lower/expr_call.rs:2077`); only the codegen arm in `crates/perry-codegen/src/expr.rs` was missing — WASM (`crates/perry-codegen-wasm/src/emit.rs:5441`) and analysis helpers (`collectors.rs:1218`, `walker.rs:786`, `analysis.rs:34`) all knew about it. Fix is a single new arm mirroring `Expr::ArrayPush` at line 3016 (same three receiver storage cases — LocalGet / boxed-captured / plain — and the same realloc-aware writeback). No new runtime helper needed: `js_array_concat(dst, src)` already existed at `crates/perry-runtime/src/array.rs:1011`, comment-as-spec'd "reserved for the internal push-spread desugaring path"; Set sources work transparently via the SET_REGISTRY check inside `js_array_concat`. **Phase 2 (V8 interop, 8 new arms)**: the LLVM backend bailed for **JsLoadModule, JsGetExport, JsCallFunction, JsCallMethod, JsGetProperty, JsSetProperty, JsNew, JsNewFromHandle** — the HIR family `perry-hir/src/js_transform.rs::transform_js_imports` produces whenever a `.ts` entry imports from a `.js` module the resolver classifies as JS-runtime-loaded (`crates/perry/src/commands/compile/collect_modules.rs:73`, extension-driven). Pre-fix the user's `bun i @codehz/pipeline` repro bombed at codegen with `JsCallFunction not yet supported`. New arms call into the existing perry-jsruntime FFI surface: `js_load_module(path_ptr, path_len) -> u64`, `js_get_export/get_property/set_property/call_function/call_method/new_instance/new_from_handle` etc. — all eight already declared in `runtime_decls.rs` except `js_call_method` (added here at line 1631 with signature `DOUBLE, &[DOUBLE, I64, I64, I64, I64]`). New shared helper `lower_js_args_array(ctx, lowered_args) -> (ptr, len)` marshals already-lowered NaN-boxed args into a stack alloca'd `[N x double]` via the issue-#167 `alloca_entry_array` pattern (hoisted to function entry block); empty input returns `("null", "0")` for the FFI's null-pointer fallback. **Module handle representation**: V8 module ids are u64; codegen returns them as f64 via `bitcast_i64_to_double` to fit `lower_expr`'s return-type contract, then consumers bitcast back to i64 before passing to the runtime. **JS value handles** are NaN-boxed f64 with V8-handle tag 0x7FFB — handled internally by perry-jsruntime's `v8_to_native` / `native_to_v8` helpers; codegen treats them as opaque doubles. **Runtime bootstrap**: new `needs_js_runtime: bool` field on `CrossModuleCtx` (threaded from `CompileOptions::needs_js_runtime`, originally set in `collect_modules.rs:105` when any `.js` module enters `ctx.js_modules`), wired into `compile_module_entry` so the entry main's prelude calls `js_runtime_init()` between `js_gc_init` and user code. Without this, every `js_load_module` site bailed at the runtime with `[js_load_module] no JS runtime state!`. **`JsCreateCallback` deliberately deferred** to Phase 2B: the runtime FFI `js_create_callback(func_ptr, closure_env, param_count)` expects `func_ptr` to have signature `(closure_env: i64, args_ptr: *const f64, args_len: i64) -> f64` (see the `native_callback_trampoline` in `crates/perry-jsruntime/src/interop.rs:993`), but Perry closure bodies have `(closure_ptr, arg0, arg1, ...)` per arity — there's no direct call-compatible mapping. Wiring this needs either codegen-emitted per-arity adapter thunks or a runtime-side closure-array dispatcher; for now the arm bails with a clear message so users see exactly what's blocked. The user's exact `pipeline()` repro at `/tmp/issue248/test.ts` now compiles + links + runs (exit 0). **Regression tests**: `test-files/test_issue_248_array_push_spread.ts` (10 cases — number/string/object arrays, empty src/dst, array-literal spread, chained push-spread, post-spread `.indexOf` + `.length`, push-spread inside loop forcing realloc past the 16-cap, mixed `push` + `push-spread` — all byte-for-byte against `node --experimental-strip-types`), plus `test-files/test_issue_248_phase2_js_interop.ts` + fixture `test-files/fixtures/issue_248_jsmod.js` exercising JsLoadModule + JsCallFunction (compile + link + clean exit). **Verified**: cargo build --release -p perry-runtime -p perry-stdlib -p perry-jsruntime -p perry clean; cargo test --release -p perry-codegen --lib 22/0; gap tests 25/28 = baseline. Bumped 0.5.366 → 0.5.368 above origin's parallel-track 0.5.366 (HarmonyOS SDK fix #250) + 0.5.367 (HarmonyOS HAP bundler #252) per the merge-collision precedent. PR #251.

## v0.5.362 — Closes #240: cross-module interface dispatch silently dropped to `undefined` when the consumer module type-imported the interface. Type-only imports are stripped at HIR lowering (`crates/perry-hir/src/lower.rs:2777`), so the interface's source module never enters `ctx.native_modules` and the consumer's `imported_classes` list ended up without the implementer. The dispatch tower at `crates/perry-codegen/src/lower_call.rs::needs_dynamic_dispatch` then found zero implementors of the called method, skipped its tower, and fell through to a generic property-get-then-closure-call that resolved `obj.method` to `undefined` (methods aren't stored as fields on instances; they're per-class symbols `_perry_method_<mod>__<Class>__<m>`). The user's bisection table pinned it to "≥4 files where the consumer is in its own module and only type-imports the interface". Fix in `crates/perry/src/commands/compile.rs`: new polymorphic-receiver augmentation pass after the existing import walk that scans every function/method/field type in the consumer's HIR for `Named(X)` references whose X doesn't resolve to any class/interface/enum/type-alias in the program HIR (`all_program_type_names` built once across `ctx.native_modules`) and isn't in a curated TS/runtime builtin set (Promise, Array, Map, Set, Date, Buffer, the typed arrays, the utility-type family, etc.). When such an unresolved name is found, the consumer pulls every program-wide exported class into `imported_classes` (existing dedup keeps it idempotent); the dispatch tower at the call site then filters per-method-name so IR size stays bounded. Without `implements`-clause tracking we can't be more surgical, but the conservative "pull everything" matches the existing precedent for namespace imports. New regression test `test-files/test_issue_240_interface_dispatch.ts` covers in-file dispatch shape; the cross-module repro is verified end-to-end in `/tmp/issue240` (4-file mongodb-style + sync variants both flip from `consume: m typeof=undefined` → `[Impl.greet] CALLED with world / consume: m typeof=string`). Pre-fix workload's `.o` had `_js_object_get_field_by_name_f64` + `_js_closure_call1` (closure-call fallback), post-fix it references `_perry_method_impl_ts__Impl__greet` directly via the dispatch tower. Gap tests 25/28 = baseline; 12-test regression set runs identically pre/post-fix.

## v0.5.361 — Issue #185 Phase D step 3: live screenshot preview in the inspector UI. The screenshot infrastructure already existed end-to-end across every platform (`perry_ui_screenshot_capture` is registered on macOS / iOS / tvOS / GTK4 / Windows / Android, the `GET /screenshot` server route already returns a PNG, the runtime has the condvar-backed cross-thread marshaling at `geisterhand_registry.rs::CaptureScreenshot`); what was missing was the inspector consuming it. **Layout change**: `crates/perry-ui-geisterhand/src/inspector_ui/index.html` switches from 2-column (`tree | detail`) to 3-column (`tree 280px | preview 1fr | detail 340px`). New `#preview` section has a top toolbar (`capture` button + `auto` checkbox + status line showing dimensions + capture timestamp) and a `#preview-frame` div with checkered transparency-grid background that holds the live `<img>`. The `capture` handler `fetch("/screenshot", { cache: "no-store" })`, blobs the response, swaps `URL.createObjectURL`'d image element into the frame, revokes the previous URL to avoid memory leaks, and updates the status line. **Selection overlay**: clicking a widget in the tree pane now also draws a translucent-bordered box on top of the preview at the widget's `frame.{x,y,w,h}` coordinates. The overlay positions itself in flex-container coordinates (handles letterboxing when the screenshot's aspect ratio doesn't match the preview frame), uses `getBoundingClientRect()` to compute the rendered img's offset, and resizes via a `window.resize` listener. **Auto-refresh checkbox** polls every 1.5s (matches the tree-refresh cadence) — off by default since screenshot capture isn't free. Status line shows e.g. `800×664 · 15:42:08`. **Verified**: `cargo build --release -p perry-ui-geisterhand` clean (1 pre-existing dead-code warning unchanged); end-to-end smoke `perry compile X.ts --enable-geisterhand && curl -o /tmp/x.png /screenshot` produces an 800×664 PNG; the inspector at `localhost:7676/` shows the screenshot inline + lets the user click any widget in the left tree to highlight its bounds on the preview. Auto-built `target/geisterhand/release/libperry_ui_geisterhand.a` (looked up first by `find_geisterhand_lib`'s search order, before `target/release/`) was overwritten with the freshly-built variant so the new HTML lands. **Phase D fully delivered**: step 1 inspector page (v0.5.349) + step 2 live style edit (v0.5.350 macOS, v0.5.360 iOS/tvOS/GTK4/Windows) + step 3 live screenshot preview here. Issue #185's three explicit asks (rich styling primitives, cross-platform parity, devtools) are now all fully shipped end-to-end. Remaining open is #230 (Win32 DirectComposition shadow — the last `Stub` in the styling matrix), Android `apply_style` dispatcher, visionOS full geisterhand registration block, and the unrelated pre-existing `ui/gallery.ts` retina baseline mismatch.
